original_text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with bare-minimum custodial oversight, no defined curriculum, untrained staff, and no educational standards or accountability measures.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with loosely defined learning goals, minimally qualified staff, high student-teacher ratios, and limited instructional materials.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with a basic standardized curriculum, staff holding entry-level credentials, and modest oversight but inconsistent quality across sites.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with an evidence-informed curriculum, certified early-childhood educators, moderate student-teacher ratios, and regular program evaluations.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with a rigorously evidence-based curriculum, highly trained and well-compensated educators, low student-teacher ratios, integrated family support services, and robust quality-assurance systems.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. federal government launches a universal pre-K education program for all 4-year-olds with a world-class, research-driven individualized curriculum, expert educators with advanced credentials and competitive salaries, very low student-teacher ratios, comprehensive wraparound services including health and nutrition support, state-of-the-art facilities, and continuous improvement driven by rigorous longitudinal outcome evaluation.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier modifies the same core scenario—a federal universal pre-K program—while progressively improving program quality dimensions: curriculum rigor, teacher qualifications and compensation, student-teacher ratios, support services, facilities, and accountability. Tier 1 describes a negligent-quality program barely beyond childcare; tier 7 describes a comprehensive, world-class educational program. Each step represents a meaningful quality improvement over the prior tier.

preference_direction: Higher tiers are more preferable because greater program quality in early childhood education leads to better developmental outcomes for children, more equitable access to high-quality learning, and stronger long-term returns on public investment. A rational agent prefers the best-quality program achievable.
